segments.s712-g17: This is a vocative/nominal phrase with no finite verb: تو (you) + تازی (Arab) addresses someone directly. بدین جنگ بر پیشدست is a prepositional/locative phrase (= "in this battle, to the forefront"); بدین is a poetic fused form meaning "with/in this," جنگ is the noun "battle," and بر پیشدست literally "toward the fore/front" indicates the role to take. The clause is elliptical (the imperative verb that follows in the next lines is implied here).
segments.s712-g18: وگر introduces an alternative conditional/contrast (poetic older form of اگر = "if/and if"). شیردل is an adjective/nickname "lion‑hearted," followed by ترک ("Turk") and خاقان پرست (a compound meaning "Khaghan‑worshipper" — خاقان = Khaghan, پرست = worshipper/suffix). The whole phrase is a noun phrase giving an alternative description of the addressee.
segments.s712-g19: بدو گفت بهرام is a common inversion: بدو = "to him/to that one" (dative), گفت functions as the past narration "said," and بهرام is the subject (Bahram). پیشی تو کن is an imperative clause: پیشی (precedence/going first) + تو کن ("you do") = "you take the lead/you go first."
segments.s712-g20: This is an interrogative: کجا = "where." پی تو افگنده‌ای is a perfect/actional phrase meaning literally "(you) have cast/set (it) after you" — here idiomatically "picked up/taken on (the notion) of following you"; افگنده‌ای is 2nd‑person perfect/past ('you have cast/laid'). این سخن = "this speech/remark."
segments.s712-g21: مقاتوره کرد is a past tense verbal phrase: مقاتوره (combat/battle) + کرد (did/engaged) = "he fought/engaged in battle." از جهاندار یاد = lit. "from/of the sovereign, remembrance" — a poetic word order meaning "remembering the sovereign" (یاد as 'remembrance' governed by از + جهاندار).
segments.s712-g22: This is a physical action: دو زاغ (numeral + noun) is the direct object; کمان را marks 'the bow' with the definite object marker را; به زه برنهاد is the verb phrase (به with target + برنهاد = archaic past 'placed upon/laid on'). Literally: "(he) placed the two zāgh of the bow upon the string." (زاغ here is a poetic/technical term tied to bow construction/imagery.)
segments.s712-g23: زه و تیر بگرفت is a simple past clause: زه و تیر (bowstring and arrow) as direct objects, بگرفت = "he seized/took hold of." شادان بدست is an adverbial phrase 'joyful/in high spirits, in (his) hand' — بدست here means 'in the hand' and qualifies his action.
segments.s712-g24: چو شد = 'when it became' (چو = when, شد = became). غرق پیکانش is a verbal/adjectival phrase 'his arrow being drenched/immersed/overwhelmed' (غرق used poetically). بگشاد شست uses an older past form (بگشاد = 'opened') + شست (thumb) — i.e. 'he spread/opened his thumb,' a small, concrete action tied to releasing/handling the bowstring. The line is compact and uses poetic diction for the shooting gesture.
segments.s712-g33: تو گفتی introduces reported speech in the past ('you said'); what follows are two imperatives presented as the content of that speech. سخن باش is an imperative using باش ('be!') idiomatically meaning 'speak' or 'be speaking'; و simply links the two commands; پاسخ شنو is another imperative (شنو = 'listen/hear') meaning 'hear the reply'. The clause thus reports a past utterance followed by two ordered commands.
segments.s712-g34: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); بشنوی is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'you hear' (the protasis of the condition). زنده مانی is the apodosis: مانی (from ماندن) is a second-person present/subjunctive/indicative meaning 'you remain' so together 'you remain alive' (زنده = alive). برو is an imperative 'go' that follows as a final command/adjunct to the conditional result. The sequence is: if you hear, you will live; (so) go.
segments.s712-g35: گزین کرد is a compound past tense ('he chose'); the verb comes first then the noun phrase jōshan گذاری خدنگ. جوشن گذاری is a verbal-noun construction meaning 'the act of putting on/placing a cuirass (armor)'; خدنگ (dagger) is the item associated with that action. Overall: 'he chose (and) the placing-on of a cuirass (and) a dagger' i.e. 'he chose armor and put on a dagger.'
segments.s712-g36: که introduces a following explanatory/relative clause ('that/so that'); آهن شدی is a past-form construction meaning 'became iron' (شد[ه] / شد + final -ی as a classical/poetic form) — i.e. turned to iron/was of iron. پیش او = 'before/in front of him' (پیش used in the sense 'in the presence of'). نرم و سنگ are two adjectives joined by و ('soft and stone-like' / 'soft and hard'), a poetic oxymoron describing the quality perceived in his presence. The clause qualifies the previously mentioned armor/weapon.
segments.s712-g37: بزد is a past-tense verb 'he struck/struck down'. بر میان is a prepositional phrase 'upon/in the middle (of)' where میان functions as 'middle'; سوار دلیر is the object 'brave rider/horseman'. Word order: verb — prepositional phrase indicating where/whom he struck — noun phrase describing the target.
segments.s712-g38: سپهبد شد is a resultative past construction 'he became spahbed (a high general)'. از introduces the cause/means ('from/because of' or 'by means of'); رزم و دینار سیر means 'sated/satisfied with battle and with dinars' — سیر here = 'sated' (having had enough), so the whole clause says he attained that rank with/through war and wealth (dinar).
segments.s712-g39: مقاتوره is the noun 'combat/fighting' and چون here is temporal 'when/as'. جنگ را with the object marker را is the definite object 'the war/battle'; برنشست is a past verb meaning 'he sat up / mounted / took his seat' (برنشستن = to seat oneself upon/onto). Together: 'When the combat rose into war, he sat up.'
segments.s712-g40: برادر is the subject 'the brother' (here likely a comrade/kinsman). دو پایش = 'his two feet' (پای 'foot' + possessive suffix ش = his). بزین (here used with بر) refers to the saddle/being on the saddle (بزین can mean 'to the saddle'/'saddle up'); بر = 'on/onto'; ببست is the past 'he bound/tied'. The sequence means 'the brother tied his two feet to the saddle.'
segments.s712-g25: بزد is a past-tense verb ('he struck/hit') placed first (verb-initial, common in epic Persian). بر is the preposition 'on/against' and governs the following noun phrase کمربند مرد سوار ('the belt of the mounted man'); مرد سوار is a tight noun + modifier sequence meaning 'mounted man' (مرد = man, سوار = mounted). The whole chunk reads 'he struck upon the mounted man's belt.'
segments.s712-g26: This is a compact, poetic clause describing the metal-on-metal clash. نسفت is an archaic/poetic word here implying a violent clash or flashing (the exact root is irregular in the lemma list); آهن ... آهن frames a typical stylistic reduplication 'iron from iron' meaning steel striking steel. از is the simple preposition 'from/of.' آبدار is used poetically of the metal (here 'well-tempered / keen'), so the phrase means roughly 'sparks flew / iron struck against iron (the keen-tempered steel).' Note: some of these classical words are poetic uses not fully reflected in the supplied lemma sense list.
segments.s712-g27: زمانی همی‌بود is a classical imperfect/continuous past: زمانی 'for a time/for a while' + همی‌بود 'was remaining/continued to be.' The name بهرام is the subject; دیر here means 'long (of time).' Altogether: 'Bahram remained for a long while.'
segments.s712-g28: که تاشد here functions as a temporal/subordinate clause 'until it came about/so that' (که + تاشد = 'until/that came to pass'). مقاتوره 'the combat/engagement' is the theme; از رزم سیر is a classical phrase meaning 'sated from battle' (سیر = satiety). So the clause means 'until the combat became sated from battle' — i.e. until the fighting subsided.
segments.s712-g29: مقاتوره is topicalized (the engagement). پنداشت is a past-tense verb 'he thought/assumed' (classical narrative uses it to report a mental act). کو شد تباه is an older phrasing meaning 'that it had become ruined' (کو here functions interrogatively/relatively in older usage, followed by شد 'became' and تباه 'ruined'). The segment reads roughly 'he supposed that the combat had been ruined.'
segments.s712-g30: خروشید is an intransitive past 'it/there cried out, roared' (a sudden loud sound or tumult). و is the simple conjunction 'and'; برگشت is past 'he/it returned/withdrew'; زان is the prepositional/relational adverb 'from that'; رزمگاه 'battlefield/place of combat.' Together: 'there was a cry/roar, and (he) withdrew from that battlefield.'
segments.s712-g31: بدو گفت is a clausal frame 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him', placed before گفت). In classical word order the indirect object may precede گفت. بهرام names the speaker; کای is the vocative 'O', and جنگجوی is the addressee 'warrior.' The whole phrase is the speech-heading: 'To him Bahram said, "O warrior..."'.
segments.s712-g32: This imperative plea contains older/contracted forms and a split object: نکشتی here is an older or poetic negative imperative/verb-form of کشیدن (sense in the supplied lemma list does not match exactly), followed by مرا/م + را (the object 'me' is split in the tokenization) = 'me'. سوی خرگه is the prepositional phrase 'toward the stable/pen.' مپوی is a negative imperative 'do not go/seeks' used to forbid movement. Together the segment functions as Bahram's command: 'Do not drag/lead me toward the stable — do not go.' Note: some tokens reflect classical contractions and the exact verb form is archaic/poetic.
segments.s712-g41: بروی اندر آمد is a poetic locution meaning “came upon/in his face/eyes” (بـِر + روی → ‘on the face’) with اندر آمد (‘came in/to’) as the verbal phrase. دو دیده پرآب is a noun phrase where دیده (‘eye/eyes’) is modified postpositively by پرآب (‘full of water/tearful’): literally “two eyes full of water (i.e. tear-filled).”
segments.s712-g42: همان is a demonstrative meaning “that/the same.” زین توری is a genitival compound ‘saddle of the Tur (Turanian saddle).’ شدش is the fused form of شد + ش (شاد/شد + possessive) meaning “it became his” or “befell him.” جای خواب is a noun–noun phrase ‘place of sleep’ = ‘sleeping place/bed.’ The whole segment states that that very Turanian saddle became his sleeping-place.
segments.s712-g43: به خاقان چنین گفت places the recipient first: به خاقان ‘to the khagan,’ چنین گفت ‘said thus’ (past tense). کای is a vocative particle “O,” and کامجوی is an epithet addressed to the addressee (‘seeker of desire’/‘aspirant’). The clause frames a direct address introduced by the verb of saying.
segments.s712-g44: همی is a durative/continuous particle often preceding verbs (here giving a general/future/continuous sense). گورکن خواهد here means ‘will be (a) gravedigger’ — گورکن ‘gravedigger’ as predicate noun and خواهد supplying the future/auxiliary sense ‘will/shall become.’ آن نامجوی points back to the person criticized ‘that name‑seeker.’
segments.s712-g45: بدو گفت خاقان is ‘to him the khagan said’ (بدو = ‘to him/unto him’). که بهتر ببین introduces an exhortation/command reported speech: که here introduces content (‘that’/‘so that’) and بهتر ببین is an idiomatic sequence ‘look more closely’ or ‘see better’ (ببین in the imperative/infinitival sense).
segments.s712-g46: کجا begins a wh‑question/adverb ‘where.’ زنده here qualifies the subject as ‘alive’ (i.e. still living). خفتست is the fused archaic present/past‑stative form ‘is asleep/has lain’ (خفت + است). بر پشت زین is a locative phrase ‘upon the back of the saddle’ (بر = on, پشت = back, زین = saddle). Together it asks/look where a living person lies asleep on the saddle’s back.
segments.s712-g47: بدو گفت بهرام sets the frame ‘to him Bahram said.’ کای is the vocative particle ‘O,’ and برمنش is an epithet ‘of noble disposition’ addressed to the addressee. Word order places the recipient/introducer before the quoted speech, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s712-g48: هم‌اکنون ‘right now’ marks immediacy. به خاک اندر آید is a verbal phrase ‘comes into the earth’ (به خاک = into the ground/earth; اندر آید = comes in), where اندر آید is the present/future‑oriented verb ‘comes/shall come.’ تنش is the noun + possessive suffix (تن + ـش) ‘his body.’ The clause states that his body will immediately go into the earth (i.e. be buried or fall lifeless).
segments.s712-g1: چوشب is a fused poetic form of چو شب ‘when night’; it introduces a temporal clause. دامن تیره is a noun phrase (‘dark hem/skirt’) describing the night as a garment, and اندر کشید is a past-tense verb phrase ‘drew in/around’ (کشیدن used transitively) with اندر marking the direction/encircling. Word order (temporal marker + image + verb) is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s712-g2: سپیده is the subject/nominative image ‘dawn’. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’, linking سپیده to its origin کوه سیه ‘the black mountain’ (کوه + adjective after it). بر دمید is a compound past verb: دمید (to blow/appear) with the prefix/particle بر adding the sense of arising or blowing forth; the whole means ‘dawn rose/breathed forth’. The clause is a simple past-event clause following the temporal clause.
segments.s712-g3: مقاتوره is an object/phrase meaning ‘battle-gear/armour’. پوشید is the simple past ‘put on/donned’. خفتان here functions as a substantive participle ‘the sleepers’ (those who had been asleep), and جنگ qualifies them (‘sleepers of war’). Persian permits this inverted order where the thing put on precedes the subject-phrase.
segments.s712-g4: بیامد is the past tense ‘came’. یکی is the indefinite ‘a certain/one’. تیغ توری is a noun phrase ‘a Turanian/Turkic sword’ (تیغ = sword; توری = of Tur/Turan). به چنگ is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘into the hand’ or ‘in hand’ (به + چنگ, here چنگ = claw/hand metaphorically). The clause describes the sudden arrival of a sword into someone’s grasp.
segments.s712-g5: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بهرام is the subject name. بشنید is the simple past ‘he heard’. بالای (literally ‘the high/up-top place’) is used as the goal or position he desires, and خواست is the past-tense verb ‘he wanted/desired’ — together meaning ‘he wished to go up’ or ‘he rose up’ in response to hearing.
segments.s712-g6: یکی جوشن is the direct object ‘one cuirass/coat of mail’ (یکی = one/a certain; جوشن = armor). خسرو آرای is an attributive compound: خسرو (Khosrow/the king) + آرای (adorned/ornaments) = ‘Khosrow-adorned’ or ‘fit for the king’, modifying جوشن. خواست is the past verb ‘he desired/asked for’. Overall: he asked for one kingly cuirass.
segments.s712-g7: گزیدند is the simple past, 3rd-person plural ('they chose'). جایی is the direct object meaning 'a place/spot' and که introduces a relative clause ('where/that'). هرگز is an adverb 'never' placed before the subject for emphasis, and پلنگ is the subject of the relative clause ('the leopard'). Word order here places the main verb (گزیدند) before its object and then attaches the relative clause that describes the chosen place.
segments.s712-g8: بر آن شخ بی‌آب is a prepositional phrase 'upon that shakh (crag/branch) waterless', where شخ is a poetic/archaic noun (see note) modified by بی‌آب 'waterless/dry'. ننهاد is the past form of نهادن with the negative prefix (here 'did not place' or 'did not set'); its understood subject is پلنگ from the previous segment. چنگ 'claw' is the thing not set down; the phrase orders the prepositional locative before the verb and places the object (چنگ) after the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s712-g49: تن دشمن تو = 'the body of your enemy' (noun + possessor; classical Persian places the possessed noun before the possessor). چنین خفته باد uses خفته (past participle/adjectival 'lying/asleep') plus the optative particle باد (‘may it be’) to express a wish about that state. Word order is poetic but the clause means 'May your enemy's body be thus asleep.'
segments.s712-g50: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'); او خفت is a simple past ('he slept' or 'he fell asleep'). بر + noun (بر اسپ توری نژاد) marks location 'upon a horse'; توری نژاد is a compound attributive 'Turanian‑bred' (classical Persian often omits a visible ezāfe in such compounds). The clause explains why/where he lay.
segments.s712-g51: فرستاد is simple past 'sent' (3rd sg.). سواری is the direct object 'a rider/horseman' placed before the verb for emphasis/poetic order; خاقان دلیر (the brave khagan) follows as the subject with دلیر as its epithet. The sequence is a typical classical/Persian poetic word order (object/verb/subject).
segments.s712-g52: به نزدیک is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to/near' (movement toward proximity). آن نامبردار شیر = 'that renowned/famed lion' — آن (that) + نامبردار (the aforementioned/renowned) modifying شیر (lion). Together this gives the destination/target of the dispatched rider.
segments.s712-g53: ورا is the enclitic/old-fashioned object pronoun 'him/it' (like او را). بسته و کشته are past participles 'bound and killed' describing that object; دیدند is past plural 'they saw'. خوار follows as a predicative adjective 'despised/humbled' commenting on the seen person. The cluster shows a typical classical ordering: object + adjectival participles + verb, with a final evaluative adjective.
segments.s712-g54: بر آسوده is an idiomatic state 'at ease/relieved' where بر marks the state and آسوده is the adjective 'relieved/at peace'. از گردش روزگار = 'from the turning(s) of time/fate' (از marks source or concern; گردش روزگار is a fixed phrase meaning vicissitudes of fortune). The phrase expresses relief regarding the course of fate.
segments.s712-g55: بخندید is simple past 'laughed/smiled'; خاقان is the subject. به دل در نهان is a set of adverbials 'in his heart, secretly' (به دل = to/into the heart; در نهان = in secret), specifying the inward, hidden nature of the laughter. The verb + adverbial sequence is straightforward classical phrasing.
segments.s712-g56: شگفت آمدش is an idiomatic impersonal/construction 'wonder came to him' = 'he was amazed' (آمدش = 'came to him'). زان is the classical contraction of از آن 'at that/because of that' and introduces the cause: زان سوار جهان = 'that rider of the world' (a lofty epithet). Together the clause means 'he was astonished at that rider.'
segments.s712-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. خاقان is the subject (the khagan). شنید is a simple past verb 'heard'. این سخن is the direct object 'these/this words/speech'. برنشست is a past tense verb composed of the prefix بر- + نشستن meaning here 'rose / sat up' (in epic style it often means he got up in response). Word order is fairly flexible/poetic: conjunction + subject + verb + object + final verb for the resulting action.
segments.s712-g10: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went / departed'. ترکان is the subject noun 'the Turks'. خسرو پرست is a compound adjective/noun 'Khosrow‑worshipping' (خسرو names the object of worship and پرست is the '-worshipper' element). Note the verb appears before the noun phrase (a poetic inversion): literally 'departed the Khosrow‑worshipping Turks.'
segments.s712-g11: بدان کار functions as a frozen prepositional phrase 'in/for that matter' (بدان = به + آن). تا introduces a temporal/clausal limit 'until' marking the span that follows. زین دو شیر دمان is a descriptive nominal group: زین here 'these' (or 'from these' depending on context), دو شیر 'two lions' (figuratively 'two brave men'), دمان is a poetic epithet (literally 'skirt/hem' but part of a compound descriptive epithet). The whole segment sets the scene: 'for that affair, until/from these two lion‑(epithet)...' — word order is compact and epic.
segments.s712-g12: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative pronoun 'who/which' (here 'who'). پیشتر خواهد آمد زمان is a periphrastic future: خواهد آمد 'will come' with پیشتر 'earlier/forward' and زمان 'time' placed after the verb for emphasis; overall: 'the time will come when one of them will come forward' (i.e. 'until the time that someone steps forward').
segments.s712-g13: مقاتوره is a poetic noun 'the combat/battle'. چون شد is a temporal phrase 'when it happened' (چون used here as 'when/as' + شد 'happened/began'). به دشت نبرد places the action's location 'on the plain (to the plain) the battle' — note the noun نبرد ('battle') and دشت ('plain') mark that the fighting took place on the plain.
segments.s712-g14: ز هامون 'from the Hamun' (ز = from/of, هامون = the marsh/expanse called Hamun). به ابر اندر آورد گرد is a poetic cluster: آورد 'brought/caused' and گرد used as a noun 'dust' (or as an element indicating a cloud of dust). Literally 'brought dust into the clouds' — idiomatically 'raised a cloud of dust' over the plain. اندر (in/into) links the motion 'brought ... into'.
segments.s712-g15: به + name is the dative 'to Bahram'. گردنکش is an epithet 'headstrong / rebellious' modifying Bahram. آواز داد is a common verbal phrase 'gave a call' = 'called out' (آواز here 'voice/call', داد is the past of دادن 'gave'). Word order is dative + addressee + epithet + verb phrase.
segments.s712-g16: که introduces reported speech 'that/asking' (here 'saying:'). اکنون 'now/at present' sets the time. ز مردی 'of a man' or 'concerning a man' (ز = about/from). چه داری 'what do you have/what do you possess (i.e. what recollection or quality do you have)' — داری is present tense 'you have'. بیاد here literally 'in memory/remembering' — together the clause asks 'Now, what do you remember/what recollection of a man do you have?'.
segments.s712-g57: پر here is a preposed adjective-like element meaning “full (of)”; it attaches to the noun اندیشه to yield “full of thought/filled with thought.” بد is a postpositive adjective modifying اندیشه (literally “thought bad” = “evil/dark thought”). تا introduces a limit/extent meaning “as far as / until,” and رسید is the simple past “reached/arrived.” The sequence therefore means “(he), full of evil thought, went until he reached the bāyvān.” Note بایوان (bāyvān) is an archaic/poetic place-name/architecture-term (iwan/portico) here.
segments.s712-g58: کلاهش is کلاه + the enclitic possessive -ش “his/its,” so this noun phrase is the subject/topic “his cap/hat.” ز شادی is an ablative/causal phrase “from/because of joy,” with ز used poetically for “from.” به کیوان رسید is a past tense clause where رسید = “reached” and کیوان (Kiwan/Saturn) is used hyperbolically/astronomically (“to the heavens”); the verb agrees with the (omitted) subject and here governs the cap as the grammatical subject.
segments.s712-g59: This is a coordinated object list followed by the past-tense verb خواست (“he wanted/asked for”). The pattern N و N خواست lists the requested items: سلیح (weapons) و درم (dinars/money). The second و links an additional item: اسپ (horse) modified by ورهی. خواست is third-person past. ورهی here is an archaic/poetic qualifier of اسپ (a descriptor of the steed); the lexical entry provided is uncertain, so its precise modern gloss is ambiguous in the text (see senses).
segments.s712-g60: همان is a demonstrative/intensifier meaning “that very / the same,” placed before تاج (crown). The conjunction و links تاج and تخت (crown and throne), and هم before تخت is an additive intensifier “also/indeed.” شاهنشهی is an adjectival/nominal compound meaning “of kingship/imperial,” qualifying تخت (i.e. ‘the imperial throne’). The whole phrase names the lofty items he demanded.
segments.s712-g61: ز here is the poetic ablative/prepositional particle “from/of.” The phrase lists sources of wealth: دینار (dinar/coin) and گوهر (gem/jewel). The token وز functions as an archaic connective parallel to و (“and”) in classical diction and links دینار to گوهر; the dictionary senses given don’t match this connective usage, so its exact sense is irregular/archaic in the entry. شاهوار is a postpositive adjective meaning “kingly/regal” modifying گوهر.
segments.s712-g62: ز again marks the source/connection “from/of.” هرگونه‌ای is an indefinite phrase “of every kind / in any manner.” آلت کار is a noun phrase ‘instrument/tool (آلت) of work/task (کار)’ — a compound describing tools or means. زار is an adjective meaning “wretched/pitiable” (used poetically here), applied to آلت کار; the phrase as a whole lists tools/means for all sorts of (even wretched) enterprises.
segments.s712-g63: فرستاده is a verbal-noun/participial construction “having sent (a) messenger” or simply “he sent a messenger.” از پیش is a fixed prepositional phrase “from before / out of the presence of,” with پیش used in the sense “in the presence of.” خاقان is the addressee/reference “the khaghan” (title). ببرد is the old/subjunctive-inflected form of بردن used here to express purpose/result (“to take / so that he take”), formed with the prefix بـ plus the root برد and functioning as the action the messenger is to perform.
segments.s712-g64: This is the main clause: سپرد is past “he entrusted/handed over.” به introduces beneficiaries/targets and locatives: به گنجور reads as a locative/goal “into the treasury/treasure-house,” and بهرام جنگی is the recipient with an epithet — Bahram described as جنگی “the warlike/warrior.” The natural reading: he placed (the treasure) in the treasury and entrusted it to Bahram the warrior.

sentences.s712-s9: You, Arab, take the lead in this battle; or, if (you are) a lion‑hearted Turk who worships the Khaghan.
sentences.s712-s10: Bahram said to him, "You go ahead; where did you pick up this talk of following you?"
sentences.s712-s11: He engaged in combat remembering the sovereign; he set the two 'zāgh' of the bow on the string.
sentences.s712-s12: He seized the bowstring and arrow, joyful in his hand; when his arrow became immersed, he opened (his) thumb.
sentences.s712-s17: You said, “Speak and hear the answer; if you hear it you will remain alive — go.”
sentences.s712-s18: He chose armor and put on a dagger; its iron became — before him it was soft and stone-like.
sentences.s712-s19: He struck at the midst of the brave horseman; he became a spahbed, sated with battle and with dinars.
sentences.s712-s20: When the fighting rose to battle he sat up; the brother bound his two feet to the saddle.
sentences.s712-s13: He struck the mounted man's belt; sparks flew as steel met steel.
sentences.s712-s14: Bahram remained a long while, until the fighting grew sated with battle.
sentences.s712-s15: He thought the combat had been ruined; there was an outcry, and he withdrew from that battlefield.
sentences.s712-s16: Bahram said to him, 'O warrior, do not drag me toward the stable; do not go.'
sentences.s712-s21: Tears came to his two eyes; that Turanian saddle became his place of sleep.
sentences.s712-s22: To the khagan he said thus: “O seeker of desire, that name-seeker will be (a) gravedigger.”
sentences.s712-s23: To him the khagan said, “Better look to see where the living lies asleep upon the saddle’s back.”
sentences.s712-s24: To him Bahram said, “O noble-minded one, his body will come down into the earth right now.”
sentences.s712-s1: When night drew its dark skirt about, dawn rose from the black mountain.
sentences.s712-s2: They donned battle-gear; the sleepers of war took up arms, and a Turkish sword came into someone’s hand.
sentences.s712-s3: When Bahram heard, he sought to go aloft; he requested one cuirass, a Khosrow-adorned breastplate.
sentences.s712-s4: They chose a spot where never had a leopard set its claw upon that waterless crag.
sentences.s712-s25: May the body of your enemy lie thus asleep; for he slept upon a Turanian-bred horse.
sentences.s712-s26: The brave khagan sent a rider near that renowned lion.
sentences.s712-s27: They saw him bound and slain, despised; (and) they were at ease about the turnings of fate.
sentences.s712-s28: The khagan laughed inwardly in his heart; he was amazed at that rider of the world.
sentences.s712-s5: When the khagan heard these words he rose; the Khosrow‑worshipping Turks departed.
sentences.s712-s6: For that affair — until the time when one of these two lion‑hearted men comes forward.
sentences.s712-s7: When the combat began on the plain, battle spread and raised a cloud of dust over the Hamun.
sentences.s712-s8: He called to Bahram the headstrong, 'Now, what recollection of a man do you have?'
sentences.s712-s29: Filled with dark thought he went until he reached the iwan; his cap, from joy, rose up to Kiwan (the heavens).
sentences.s712-s30: He demanded weapons and dinars and a war-horse — and that very crown and that throne of kingship.
sentences.s712-s31: From dinars and from jewels, regal and lofty; for every kind, instruments (tools) of work, wretched (or pitiable).
sentences.s712-s32: He sent a messenger to take (it) from before the khaghan; he put it in the treasury and entrusted it to Bahram the warrior.
